scores.materiality_reasoning: Distraint involves the physical seizure of tangible property and wealth.
scores.generality_reasoning: Specific to the recovery of debts owed to the state by tax contractors.
scores.persistence_reasoning: The loss of property through distraint is a permanent structural wealth change.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Tax farming is a chosen occupation and commercial role requiring specific capital.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes Athenian statutes active during the trial in 399 BCE.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Cites a specific, verifiable legal procedure for debt recovery as fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: The definition of a tax farmer does not inherently include losing all property.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Atimia (disfranchisement) removes the material right to hold office and conduct legal suits.
scores.generality_reasoning: Civic rights constitute a foundational societal axis for Athenian men.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Historical Athenian atimia was generally a lifelong status unless specifically repealed.
scores.immutability_reasoning: The group is defined by a specific behavioral event (military desertion).
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to standing Athenian law regarding military misconduct.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Lists specific legal categories of offenders subject to a known penalty.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Desertion is an act, not a group defined by lack of rights.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Loss of civic rights impacts the ability to participate in the material benefits of the state.
scores.generality_reasoning: Full civic rights are the primary broad category of participation in the polis.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Atimia for parental maltreatment was a permanent legal status in Athens.
scores.immutability_reasoning: The group is defined by the behavior of maltreating parents.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes active Athenian laws governing familial duties and civic standing.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reports a specific statute (the law of maltreatment) and its mandated penalty.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Maltreating a parent does not by definition mean being disfranchised.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Right to speak in the Assembly is a social role involving public influence.
scores.generality_reasoning: The right to speak (isegoria) was a major recognizable political right.
scores.persistence_reasoning: The restriction lasted as long as the post-coup reconciliation decrees were in force.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Membership in the Four Hundred's forces was a historical/political role.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Discusses the legal status of specific groups in the immediate aftermath of 403 BCE.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Describes a specific, documented exception in the restoration decrees.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a soldier for a regime doesn't inherently mean being banned from speaking later.

scores.materiality_reasoning: The right to propose measures has direct material effects on state policy.
scores.generality_reasoning: Active political participation is a foundational societal axis.
scores.persistence_reasoning: The restoration of rights was intended to be a permanent status change.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizenship is a structural birth-status requiring legal recognition.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Relates to the decree of Patrocleides in force during the author's youth.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reports a specific decree while framing it within a larger argument.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Citizenship does not automatically guarantee rights for those in debt.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Protection from individualized laws prevents personal property seizure or banishment.
scores.generality_reasoning: Isolates a foundational principle of the rule of law and equality.
scores.persistence_reasoning: This is a structural constitutional rule intended to be permanent.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Applies to the body of citizens, a stable legal birth-group.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Quotes a standing law of the restored Athenian democracy.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Verbatim cites a specific statute with numeric procedural requirements.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Citizenship as a status does not logically require protection from ad hominem laws.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Prevents magistrates from material interference based on unwritten rules.
scores.generality_reasoning: Covers a broad category of legal enforcement and administrative power.
scores.persistence_reasoning: A structural limit on executive power holding for the life of the citizen.
scores.immutability_reasoning: The group 'Citizens' is a stable legally defined group.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Part of the legal reform under the Archonship of Eucleides.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Cites a specific, well-known constitutional rule of the Athenian state.
scores.tautology_reasoning: The definition of citizen does not include having a codified law system.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Ensures the material ability to collect debts and hold onto property via contracts.
scores.generality_reasoning: Governs the broad domain of private civil law and economic transactions.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Judicial decisions on property remain valid and binding for life.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Citizenship in Athens was a stable, hereditary legal status.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Relates to the active legal framework post-403 BCE.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Cites the specific legal terms of the reconciliation agreement.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Citizenship does not automatically imply the validity of past court cases.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Amnesty protects individuals from material penalties like fines or execution.
scores.generality_reasoning: Covers a broad class of past public offenses and legal liability.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Applies to all acts before a specific date, creating a permanent legal shield.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Applies to the citizen body (excluding specific exiles).
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: The amnesty was the foundational contemporary peace of 403 BCE.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Refers to the specific, historic statute of the amnesty of Eucleides.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a citizen does not require being immune from past crimes.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Close confinement/imprisonment is a direct material restriction on the body.
scores.generality_reasoning: Tied specifically to the failure to pay for a particular tax-farming contract.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Imprisonment lasts until the specific debt is settled.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Defined by the act of failing to pay a specific state debt.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes the legal risks faced by contemporary tax-farming professionals.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reports a specific power of the Council as laid down by law.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Defaulting on a tax does not mean 'imprisonment' by definition.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Protection from summary execution and loss of goods are maximum material concern.
scores.generality_reasoning: Specific to individuals holding office during anti-democratic regimes.
scores.persistence_reasoning: The status of 'outlaw' was effectively permanent once established.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Defined by the action of holding public office under an oligarchy.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Cites the Decree of Demophantus, active in the restored democracy.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Verbatim cites a specific inscribed law/decree verbatim.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being an official does not inherently mean being an outlaw.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Capital punishment is the most extreme material consequence.
scores.generality_reasoning: Tied to a single niche ritual act in a specific temple.
scores.persistence_reasoning: The consequence (death) is permanent.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Defined by the single behavioral act of placing a bough incorrectly.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: The author is being tried under this 'ancient law' in his present.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reports it as a law while acknowledging its controversial 'interpretation'.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Placing a bough is an act, not inherently a capital crime.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Exemption from prosecution avoids materials effects like fines or exile.
scores.generality_reasoning: Covers all legal procedures based on laws from before a certain date.
scores.persistence_reasoning: The voiding of old laws was a structural, permanent change to legal reality.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Applies to the body of citizens relative to the law.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Refers to the constitutional settlement under which the author now lives.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Cites the fundamental rule of the Archonship of Eucleides as fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: The definition of citizen does not include the expiration dates of laws.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Right to hold property and live in the city are material foundations.
scores.generality_reasoning: Restores a broad suite of civic and personal status rights.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Status as a restored citizen was meant to be a permanent reintegration.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Restored exiles were a group whose legal status was birth-contingent (citizenship).
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes the recent and ongoing effects of the 403 BCE reconciliation.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Describes specific political/legal acts taken by the Athenian people.
scores.tautology_reasoning: An 'exile' is not by definition someone who is 'restored'.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Right to give 'exegesis' (interpretation) is a high-status social role.
scores.generality_reasoning: Limited to the specific practice of sacred law interpretation.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Hereditary prerogative belonging to the clan for life.
scores.immutability_reasoning: The Eumolpidae are a hereditary noble clan/lineage.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Relates to the active religious hierarchy in Athens at the time.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: States the 'prerogative' as a matter of established sacred law.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a Eumolpid doesn't linguistically mean 'interpreter' (though historically linked).

scores.materiality_reasoning: Inheritance of land/estate is a core material wealth entitlement.
scores.generality_reasoning: The epiklerate was a foundational axis of Athenian family and property law.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Determines the lifelong property status and marital life of the woman.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Daughters without brothers are defined by birth and family structure.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Standing law in Athens used regularly by citizens.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Explains the legal situation and mechanism of the 'epikleros' as fact.
scores.tautology_reasoning: A brotherless daughter is not by definition a property-inheritor in all systems.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Registration grants the child material rights to inheritance and citizenship.
scores.generality_reasoning: Broadly concerns the legal personhood and legitimacy of offspring.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Registration is a one-time act with lifelong structural consequences.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Lineage and the role of father are immutable/strictly birth-based.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes standard Athenian phratry practices at the Apaturia.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Describes the specific legal vote and oath required for registration.
scores.tautology_reasoning: A father is not by definition someone who registers a child.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Funding liturgies involves significant direct material expenditure.
scores.generality_reasoning: Liturgies were a common, broad system of public financing in Athens.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Liturgies were recurring annual or periodic obligations during wealth years.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Wealth brackets are durable but individuals can move in or out of them.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Andocides is listing liturgies he performed recently.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Names specific offices and financial roles as legal obligations.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being wealthy does not by definition mean paying for a state festival.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Protection from capital punishment is a direct bodily material resource.
scores.generality_reasoning: Concerns specific outcomes in a criminal trial.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Applies to the single episode of a jury's verdict in a trial.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Nobility/ancestry is strictly immutable birth-fixed status.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: The plea is made to a contemporary Athenian jury in his trial.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: A rhetorical claim and 'right' asserted as a plea for mercy, not a statute.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Nobility does not linguistically entail being pardoned for crimes.

scores.materiality_reasoning: Citizenship grants material rights like property ownership and voting.
scores.generality_reasoning: Foundational change in legal personhood across all societal domains.
scores.persistence_reasoning: Naturalization is a lifelong structural status change.
scores.immutability_reasoning: Refers to ethnic groups (Thessalians/Andrians) undergoing legal change.
scores.contemporarity_reasoning: Describes contemporary wartime measures taken by the Athenian state.
scores.opinion_vs_fact_reasoning: Reports a current policy shift used as a benchmark for his argument.
scores.tautology_reasoning: Being a foreigner from Thessaly does not mean being an Athenian citizen.
